facts.text: The Arts and Crafts movement originated in Britain in the 1860s as a reaction against industrialization and mass production.
A standard crochet hook ranges from 2mm (size B) to 19mm (size S), with larger hooks creating looser, more open stitches.
Isaac Singer didn't invent the sewing machine, but his company popularized it with the first installment payment plan in 1856.
Raku pottery involves removing pieces from the kiln while still glowing hot and placing them in combustible materials for dramatic effects.
The word 'origami' comes from the Japanese words 'ori' (folding) and 'kami' (paper).
A dovetail joint is considered one of the strongest and most attractive woodworking joints, used for centuries in furniture drawers.
Etsy was founded in 2005 in a Brooklyn apartment and now has over 90 million active buyers worldwide.
Soy wax candles burn 30-50% longer than traditional paraffin wax candles and produce less soot.
The Cricut machine was originally designed in 2006 for scrapbooking but has evolved into a versatile cutting tool for hundreds of materials.
Leather tanning has been practiced for over 7,000 years, making it one of humanity's oldest crafts.
William Morris, leader of the Arts and Crafts movement, designed over 600 patterns for wallpaper, textiles, and embroidery.
Cross-stitch is one of the oldest forms of embroidery, with examples dating back to the 6th century in Egypt.
A pottery wheel can spin at speeds between 25 and 300 RPM, with most potters preferring 40-80 RPM for throwing.
Martha Stewart launched her first magazine, Martha Stewart Living, in 1990, helping popularize DIY home crafts.
The Japanese art of kintsugi repairs broken pottery with gold, celebrating imperfection rather than hiding it.
Hot glue guns were invented in the 1940s and originally used in industrial shoe manufacturing before becoming a craft staple.
Quilting bees were important social gatherings in colonial America where women would collectively stitch quilts.
Wire wrapping jewelry techniques date back to ancient Sumerian goldsmiths around 2000 BCE.
Papier-mâché literally means 'chewed paper' in French and has been used since ancient China for making helmets and pot lids.
The router is often called the most versatile tool in a woodworking shop, capable of cutting profiles, grooves, and joints.
National Craft Month is celebrated every March in the United States to encourage creative arts.
Cold process soap making involves mixing lye (sodium hydroxide) with oils and requires 4-6 weeks of curing time.
Ravelry, the social networking site for knitters and crocheters, has over 9 million registered members.
Bisque firing in ceramics typically reaches temperatures of 1700-1900°F (927-1038°C) to harden greenware.
The first Joann Fabric store opened in Cleveland, Ohio in 1943 as a single cheese shop that transitioned to fabric.
Yarn bombing, also called guerrilla knitting, involves covering public structures with knitted or crocheted material as street art.
A mortise and tenon joint has been used in woodworking for thousands of years and was found in ancient Egyptian furniture.
UV resin cures in minutes under ultraviolet light, making it popular for quick jewelry projects and coatings.
The Singer sewing machine company was the first American multinational corporation, with factories worldwide by the 1860s.
Balsa wood is the lightest commercial wood, weighing only about 7.5 pounds per cubic foot, making it ideal for model building.
The maker movement, emphasizing DIY technology and crafting, gained mainstream attention around 2005 with the launch of Make magazine.
Venetian glass beads, known as trade beads, were used as currency in Africa and the Americas from the 15th century onward.
A kiln can reach temperatures over 2300°F (1260°C) for high-fire stoneware and porcelain ceramics.
The Bauhaus school, founded in 1919 in Germany, merged fine arts with crafts and influenced modern design worldwide.
Calligraphy literally means 'beautiful writing' and has been practiced as an art form in cultures worldwide for millennia.
Upcycling, or creative reuse, transforms waste materials into new products of higher quality or environmental value.
Merino wool is considered the gold standard for knitting yarn due to its softness, warmth, and lack of itchiness.
The table saw is considered the centerpiece of most woodworking shops and accounts for the most workshop injuries.
Scrapbooking became a mainstream hobby in the 1990s and grew into a multi-billion dollar industry in the United States.
Vegetable-tanned leather uses tree bark tannins and takes weeks to months to produce, versus hours for chrome tanning.
HGTV launched in 1994 and popularized DIY home improvement projects, reaching over 80 million households.
The French knitting technique, also called spool knitting, uses a small wooden spool with nails to create a cord of knitted yarn.
Stained glass windows date back to at least the 7th century and became a major art form in medieval Gothic cathedrals.
Polymer clay (like Sculpey and Fimo) is actually a PVC-based modeling compound, not true clay, and cures in a home oven.
A coping saw, with its thin blade and U-shaped frame, is essential for cutting intricate curves in woodworking.
The first World Origami Day was celebrated on November 11, 2005, honoring the birthday of origami master Lillian Oppenheimer.
Melt and pour soap making is the easiest method for beginners since it doesn't require handling lye.
Pinterest, launched in 2010, became the go-to platform for DIY project inspiration, with crafts being one of its top categories.
Japanese sashiko embroidery originated as a practical stitching technique to reinforce and repair worn clothing.
Cherry, walnut, and maple are considered the 'holy trinity' of North American furniture woods.
The lost wax casting technique for jewelry and metalwork has been used for over 5,000 years.
Acrylic yarn was invented in the 1940s by DuPont and became popular because it was affordable, washable, and hypoallergenic.
A leather burnisher smooths and seals the rough edges of cut leather, creating a polished, professional finish.
The first Michaels craft store opened in Dallas, Texas in 1973 and now has over 1,200 locations across North America.
Decoupage, the art of decorating objects with paper cutouts, comes from the French word 'découper' meaning to cut out.
Hand-thrown pottery is considered 'thrown' because the clay was historically 'thrown' or pushed onto the spinning wheel.
National Quilting Day is celebrated on the third Saturday of March in the United States.
The craft of macramé, involving decorative knotting, experienced a massive revival in the 2010s home décor trend.
Beeswax candles are the oldest type of candle, dating back to ancient Egypt and Rome.
A wood lathe spins a piece of wood while a craftsperson shapes it with cutting tools, a technique called wood turning.
The Friendship Bracelet craze of the 1980s-90s used a form of macramé knotting with embroidery floss.
Epoxy resin art, including river tables and geode designs, became a massive trend in the late 2010s.
The bobbin lace technique originated in Italy in the 15th century and spread throughout Europe as a luxury craft.
Inkle looms are small, portable looms used for weaving narrow bands and straps, popular with medieval reenactors.
Washi tape, the decorative masking tape used in crafts, originated in Japan and means 'Japanese paper.'
A swage block is a heavy metal tool used in blacksmithing and metalworking to form curves and shapes.
The first electric sewing machine was patented in 1889 by the Singer company.
Terrazzo, a composite material of marble chips set in concrete, was invented by Venetian construction workers in the 15th century.
Hand-dyed yarn, known as indie-dyed yarn, has become a thriving small business category on platforms like Etsy.
A spokeshave is an ancient woodworking tool used to shape and smooth curved surfaces, originally used for making wheel spokes.
Needle felting uses barbed needles to interlock wool fibers without spinning or weaving, creating 3D sculptures.
The quilting technique called 'paper piecing' (or English Paper Piecing) uses paper templates for precise geometric shapes.
Saddle stitching is the strongest hand-sewing technique for leather, using two needles simultaneously from opposite sides.
Pyrography, the art of decorating wood with burn marks, has been practiced since ancient times using heated metal tools.
The craft glitter industry produces over 10 million pounds of glitter annually, mostly using aluminum-coated PET plastic.
Wedgwood pottery, founded by Josiah Wedgwood in 1759, is still one of the most recognized ceramics brands worldwide.
A brad nailer fires small, thin nails ideal for delicate trim work and craft projects without splitting the wood.
The average American quilter spends over $3,000 per year on quilting supplies and fabrics.
Batik is an ancient wax-resist dyeing technique originating in Java, Indonesia, now recognized as a UNESCO cultural heritage.
3D printing pens allow crafters to draw three-dimensional objects freehand using heated plastic filament.
The craft of basket weaving is considered one of the oldest human crafts, with fragments dating back 10,000-12,000 years.
Gesso is a white primer applied to canvases and craft surfaces to prepare them for painting and mixed media.
Circular knitting needles, connected by a flexible cable, allow knitters to work in the round without seams.
Shellac, a natural resin secreted by lac bugs, was the original wood finish before polyurethane became popular.
The first Hobby Lobby store opened in Oklahoma City in 1972, starting with a $600 loan and a 300-square-foot space.
Slip casting in ceramics involves pouring liquid clay (slip) into plaster molds to create uniform shapes.
A rotary cutter, invented in 1979 by Olfa, revolutionized quilting by allowing fast, precise fabric cutting.
Mod Podge, the all-in-one decoupage sealer, was invented in 1967 and remains one of the most popular craft supplies.
Japanese kumihimo braiding uses a weighted disk (marudai) to create round or flat braids from multiple strands.
The dovetail saw, with its fine teeth and stiff back, is specifically designed for cutting precise dovetail joints.
Punch needle embroidery creates a looped, textured surface by punching yarn through fabric with a hollow needle.
Candle wicks are typically made of braided cotton and must be matched to the candle diameter for proper burning.
The Arts and Crafts Exhibition Society held its first show in London in 1888.
A bench chisel set (1/4", 1/2", 3/4", and 1") covers most woodworking needs for hand-cut joinery.
Tunisian crochet, also called Afghan stitch, uses an elongated hook and combines elements of knitting and crochet.
Lino cutting (linocut) printmaking was popularized by Henri Matisse and Pablo Picasso in the early 20th century.
E6000 adhesive, a favorite among jewelry makers, was originally developed as an industrial-strength adhesive.
The craft of tatting uses a small shuttle or needle to create intricate lace from a series of knots and loops.
Chalk paint, popularized by Annie Sloan in 1990, allows furniture painting without sanding or priming first.
A pottery rib tool, usually made of wood or rubber, smooths the walls of pots while they spin on the wheel.
FIMO polymer clay was invented in 1939 by Käthe Kruse, a German dollmaker, and named by her daughter Sophie.
The world's largest ball of yarn is in Cawker City, Kansas, measuring over 45 feet in circumference.
A miter saw cuts precise angles for frames, molding, and trim work, with most models offering 0-45 degree cuts.
Resin art requires careful measurement of a two-part mixture (resin and hardener) in exact ratios for proper curing.
The Guild of American Papercutters preserves the art of scherenschnitte, the German tradition of paper cutting.
A serger (overlock machine) trims, encloses, and finishes seam edges in a single pass, essential for professional garments.
Japanese urushi lacquerwork uses tree sap that hardens into an incredibly durable, glossy finish on wood and other materials.
The craft of marquetry applies thin veneers of different woods to create pictorial or geometric patterns on furniture.
Rit dye, first manufactured in 1917, is one of the oldest and most widely available fabric dyes for home crafters.
A kiln sitter is a mechanical device that automatically shuts off an electric kiln when the correct temperature is reached.
Arm knitting, which uses your arms instead of needles, can produce a chunky blanket in about 45 minutes.
The jigsaw puzzle was invented around 1760 by John Spilsbury, a London mapmaker who mounted maps on wood and cut them.
Leatherworking awls punch holes for stitching and come in diamond, round, and overstitch prong varieties.
National Scrapbooking Day is celebrated on the first Saturday of May.
A router table turns a handheld router into a stationary tool, improving precision for edge profiling and joinery.
Felting (wet felting) works because wool fibers have microscopic scales that lock together when agitated with heat and moisture.
The glue gun was patented in 1949 and became widely available to crafters in the 1970s and 1980s.
Sgraffito is a ceramic decoration technique where a top layer of slip is scratched away to reveal the clay color beneath.
The Maker Faire, the largest celebration of DIY culture, was first held in San Mateo, California in 2006.
Chain mail jewelry uses the same interlocking ring technique once used to make medieval armor.
Warp and weft are the two basic components of weaving—warp runs vertically and weft runs horizontally.
A dust collection system is considered essential in any woodworking shop to prevent respiratory issues from fine wood dust.
Diamond painting combines cross-stitch and paint-by-numbers, using tiny resin 'diamonds' on an adhesive canvas.
The Jacquard loom, invented in 1804, used punched cards to automate complex weaving patterns and inspired early computers.
Alcohol ink art creates vibrant, fluid abstract designs on non-porous surfaces like Yupo paper and ceramic tiles.
A block plane is the smallest bench plane in woodworking, ideal for trimming end grain and fitting joints.
Soap stamps and soap bevelers give handmade soap bars their characteristic artisan appearance.
The tradition of making friendship bracelets originated with indigenous peoples of Central and South America.
Embossing in paper crafts uses heat and special powder to create raised, glossy designs on cardstock.
Bernard Leach, known as the 'Father of British studio pottery,' bridged Eastern and Western ceramic traditions.
Grout is used in mosaic art to fill the spaces between tesserae (small pieces of tile, glass, or stone).
The craft supply chain was severely disrupted during 2020, causing shortages of fabric, yarn, and sewing machines.
A biscuit joiner (plate joiner) cuts crescent-shaped slots for hidden biscuit joints in woodworking panels.
Pour painting (acrylic pouring) creates marbled effects by mixing acrylic paint with pouring medium and silicone oil.
Kumihimo, the Japanese art of braid-making, produces round cords often used in jewelry and decorative trim.
A leather skiving knife thins the edges of leather pieces so they can be folded or overlapped without bulk.
The world's oldest known piece of knitting dates to around the 11th century CE, found in Egypt.
Sublimation printing transfers dye onto mugs, shirts, and other items using heat and pressure.
A pottery kiln shelf, made of silicon carbide or cordierite, supports pieces during firing and can last for years.
World Card Making Day is celebrated on the first Saturday of October.
Whittling, the art of carving shapes from wood with a knife, requires only a sharp blade and a piece of softwood.
Seed beads, the tiny glass beads used in beadwork, are measured in 'aught' sizes—the higher the number, the smaller the bead.
Mod Podge comes in matte, gloss, satin, outdoor, fabric, and even dishwasher-safe formulas.
A hand plane is considered one of the most satisfying woodworking tools, producing thin, curled shavings.
Tie-dye experienced massive revivals in the 1960s counterculture and again during the 2020 stay-at-home period.
The Bernina sewing machine company has been family-owned in Switzerland since 1893.
Celadon glaze, known for its jade-green color, was perfected in ancient China during the Song Dynasty.
A craft room is one of the most desired dedicated spaces in home design, according to National Association of Home Builders surveys.
Latch hook rug making creates thick pile rugs by knotting short pieces of yarn through a mesh canvas.
OOAK (One Of A Kind) is a common term on Etsy and at craft shows to describe unique, handmade items.
Japanese washi paper is made from plant fibers (kozo, mitsumata, or gampi) and is stronger than regular wood-pulp paper.
The band saw is ideal for cutting curves in thick wood and is safer than a table saw for many operations.
Thomas Chippendale, one of history's most famous furniture makers, published 'The Gentleman and Cabinet-Maker's Director' in 1754.
Candle fragrance load should typically be 6-10% of the wax weight for a properly scented candle.
The craft of découpage was fashionable in 18th-century France and Italy as a way to imitate expensive lacquerwork.
A drop spindle is the oldest known tool for spinning fiber into yarn, used for thousands of years before the spinning wheel.
Dremel rotary tools are popular among crafters for carving, engraving, sanding, and polishing on small projects.
The tradition of quilting in America is believed to have arrived with Dutch and English settlers in the 17th century.
Terracotta literally means 'baked earth' in Italian and refers to unglazed, low-fire clay pottery.
Heat transfer vinyl (HTV) is cut on machines like Cricut or Silhouette and applied to fabrics with a heat press.
A workbench vise is essential for woodworking, holding pieces firmly for sawing, planing, and chiseling.
The Craftsman style of architecture and furniture, popular in early 1900s America, was inspired by the Arts and Crafts movement.
World Knitting Day is celebrated on the second Saturday of June.
Amigurumi is the Japanese art of knitting or crocheting small stuffed animals and creatures.
A craft heat gun operates at 300-700°F and is used for embossing, shrink wrap, candle making, and resin work.
Beadwork by Native American artisans is one of the most intricate and culturally significant craft traditions in North America.
A pottery wedging table with a plaster top absorbs moisture and prepares clay for throwing by removing air bubbles.
Wax melts (wickless candles) became popular in the 2000s as a flameless alternative to traditional candles.
A dado blade set allows a table saw to cut wide, flat-bottomed grooves for shelving and cabinetry.
Cross-stitch patterns use a grid system where each square represents one stitch, similar to pixel art.
The Silhouette Cameo cutting machine, Cricut's main competitor, was introduced in 2011 and also cuts vinyl, paper, and fabric.
Gustav Stickley, a leading figure in the American Arts and Crafts movement, published 'The Craftsman' magazine from 1901-1916.
Leather can be dyed using oil-based, water-based, or alcohol-based dyes, each producing different finishes.
A Forstner bit drills clean, flat-bottomed holes in wood, essential for hardware installation and joinery.
National Sewing Month is celebrated every September in the United States.
Quilling, the art of rolling thin strips of paper into decorative shapes, dates back to Renaissance-era European nuns.
A kiln pyrometric cone (Orton cone) bends at a specific temperature, indicating when the kiln has reached the right heat.
Bob Ross's 'The Joy of Painting' aired from 1983 to 1994 and inspired millions to try painting as a hobby.
Japanese shibori dyeing uses various folding, twisting, and binding techniques to create resist patterns in indigo dye.
A scroll saw uses a thin reciprocating blade to cut intricate curves and patterns in wood, metal, and other materials.
Annie Sloan's Chalk Paint, introduced in 1990, created an entire cottage industry of furniture upcycling.
Cyanotype printing, which produces blueprints, is also used as a craft technique on fabric and paper using sunlight.
A lathe chuck holds workpieces for wood turning and comes in three-jaw and four-jaw configurations.
The first YouTube craft tutorial videos appeared around 2006-2007, eventually creating full-time careers for creators.
Gouge chisels have curved cutting edges and are essential for wood carving and turning decorative profiles.
International Scrapbooking Industry Day is celebrated on March 4.
A bone folder is a flat, smooth tool made from bone or Teflon used to crease and fold paper precisely in bookbinding and card making.
Encaustic painting uses heated beeswax mixed with colored pigments, a technique dating back to ancient Greece.
The craft of stenciling was used in ancient cave art and remains popular for home décor and fabric printing.
A bench grinder sharpens chisels, plane blades, and other edged tools essential to woodworking.
Etsy sellers shipped over $13 billion worth of goods in 2022, with handmade crafts being a core category.
Terrarium building combines gardening and crafting, creating miniature ecosystems in glass containers.
A leather stamping tool impresses patterns into dampened vegetable-tanned leather using a mallet.
Die cutting machines stamp out shapes from paper, felt, vinyl, and thin chipboard for crafting projects.
The purl stitch, one of the two fundamental knitting stitches, creates a bumpy texture and is the reverse of the knit stitch.
A combination square is an essential layout tool in woodworking for marking 90° and 45° angles.
Martha Stewart's first craft book, 'Entertaining,' was published in 1982 and launched her homemaking empire.
Porcelain, also called 'white gold,' was first developed in China during the Tang Dynasty around 618-907 CE.
A brayer roller is used in printmaking and paper crafts to evenly apply ink or adhesive to surfaces.
The average Cricut machine owner buys over $200 in materials and accessories in their first year.
Chipcarving is a woodworking technique that removes small chips of wood to create geometric patterns.
Kumiko is the Japanese art of assembling small wooden pieces without nails into delicate geometric patterns for screens and panels.
The craft of candle dipping involves repeatedly dipping a wick into melted wax to build up layers, a method used since medieval times.
A Potter's wheel was first developed in Mesopotamia around 3500 BCE, making pottery one of the world's oldest crafts.
The fiber arts include knitting, crocheting, weaving, spinning, felting, and dyeing—all working with yarn or fiber.
A bevel gauge (sliding T-bevel) transfers and marks angles in woodworking that aren't 90° or 45°.
National Embroidery Month is celebrated every February in the United States.
A pottery trimming tool carves away excess clay from the bottom of leather-hard pots to create a foot ring.
Loom knitting uses a circular or rectangular loom with pegs, making knitting accessible for people with hand dexterity issues.
The first craft fair in America is believed to have been held in the late 1800s as part of agricultural county fairs.
Plaster of Paris molds are used in ceramics for slip casting and in crafts for casting resin and concrete objects.
A marking gauge scores a line parallel to an edge in woodworking, essential for layout and joinery.
Sam Maloof, an American woodworker, was famous for his sculptural rocking chairs and became a MacArthur Fellow.
The weaver's knot is the most commonly used knot for joining broken warp threads on a loom.
Precious metal clay (PMC) contains real silver or gold particles in an organic binder that burns away during firing.
A pocket hole jig (like the Kreg system) creates strong, hidden joints for furniture and cabinetry in minutes.
The art of glass fusing melts multiple pieces of glass together in a kiln at around 1,300-1,500°F.
Boro stitching is a Japanese textile mending technique that layers and patches fabric with visible running stitches.
A rotary tool (Dremel) with a flex shaft attachment allows precision carving and engraving in tight spaces.
The Silhouette and Cricut machines can now cut over 300 different materials, from balsa wood to faux leather.
Greenware is the term for unfired pottery that is bone dry but extremely fragile and can still be recycled.
A woodworking hand saw with 10-12 teeth per inch (TPI) is versatile enough for most crosscutting tasks.
World Quilting Day began in 1991 to celebrate the art, history, and heritage of quilt making.
Candle tunneling occurs when only the center of a candle melts down, caused by not burning long enough on the first use.
A featherboard holds wood against the fence or table during routing and sawing, preventing kickback.
Zentangle is a meditative drawing method using structured patterns, created by Rick Roberts and Maria Thomas in 2003.
The Navajo loom is a traditional upright loom used by Navajo weavers to create intricate blankets and rugs.
A leather edge beveler shaves the sharp corners off cut leather edges before burnishing for a rounded finish.
The modern scrapbook evolved from 'commonplace books' kept by writers and scholars since the Renaissance.
Stoneware clay matures at cone 6-10 (around 2200-2380°F) and is more durable than earthenware.
A half-lap joint is one of the simplest woodworking joints, where half the thickness of each piece is removed to create a flush fit.
Casting resin needs to be poured in layers for thick pieces to prevent excessive heat buildup (exothermic reaction).
I Love Yarn Day is celebrated on the second Saturday of October.
The craft of bookbinding has been practiced since at least the 2nd century CE, with techniques evolving through the centuries.
A random orbital sander moves in both a circular and orbital pattern, preventing swirl marks on wood surfaces.
The first sewing pattern company, Butterick, was founded in 1863 by Ebenezer Butterick.
Wet-on-wet (alla prima) oil painting technique was popularized for hobbyists by Bob Ross on television.
A craft organizer with labeled bins, stacking containers, and pegboard walls can transform a small space into a functional studio.
Japanese chiyogami paper, with its bright patterns and gold accents, is prized for origami and paper crafts.
A cabinet scraper is a thin, flat piece of steel that produces a smooth finish on wood without sandpaper.
The tradition of creating worry dolls—tiny handmade dolls from Guatemala—persists as a beloved craft.
A pottery throwing bat is a disc that attaches to the wheel head, making it easy to remove wet pots without distortion.
Sashiko thread is thicker than regular embroidery floss and gives sashiko embroidery its characteristic bold appearance.
National DIY Day is celebrated on April 2 in the United States.
Pewter casting is one of the most accessible metalsmithing techniques because pewter has a very low melting point of 338-446°F.
A miter box holds a saw at precise angles for cutting molding, picture frames, and small trim pieces.
The color wheel is essential for crafters working with paint, yarn, fabric, or beads to create harmonious color combinations.
Lye (sodium hydroxide) is essential for cold process soap but must be handled with gloves, goggles, and proper ventilation.
A wood moisture meter helps prevent warping and cracking by ensuring lumber is properly dried before use.
The Japanese craft of temari involves embroidering geometric patterns on thread-wrapped balls.
A pounce wheel transfers pattern markings through paper onto fabric or other surfaces for embroidery and sewing.
The world record for the longest crochet chain is over 80 miles, set by Anne Vanier-Drüssel.
Clamps are often called the 'third hand' of woodworking—most shops have dozens in various sizes and styles.
A gum eraser and kneaded eraser are essential tools for artists working in charcoal, pastel, and graphite.
The Bauhaus school was closed by the Nazis in 1933, but its design principles continue to influence crafts and design worldwide.
A leather lacing chisel punches evenly spaced holes for lacing leather pieces together instead of stitching.
Paint pouring techniques include dirty pour, flip cup, swipe, and Dutch pour, each creating different effects.
A brad point drill bit creates clean, precise holes in wood without wandering, unlike standard twist bits.
Glycerin soap bases are translucent and allow crafters to embed flowers, toys, and decorations inside.
The Japanese art of kirie involves cutting intricate designs from a single sheet of paper using a craft knife.
A crosscut sled on a table saw improves accuracy and safety when cutting boards to precise lengths.
Norman Rockwell's illustrations, while fine art, inspired countless craft reproductions and cross-stitch patterns.
A pottery glaze is essentially a thin layer of glass that forms on the surface of ceramics during firing.
Yarn weight categories range from 0 (lace) to 7 (jumbo), standardized by the Craft Yarn Council.
A palm router (trim router) weighs about 3 pounds and is perfect for chamfering edges and cutting small grooves.
World Creativity and Innovation Day is celebrated on April 21.
A leather pricking iron marks stitch hole locations evenly before punching through with an awl.
Toile de Jouy is an 18th-century French fabric pattern showing pastoral scenes, popular in quilting and home décor.
A shooting board is a woodworking jig that holds wood at precise angles for trimming end grain with a hand plane.
Copa-cobana soap technique creates swirled color patterns by pouring thin layers of soap at different angles.
Pysanky is the Ukrainian art of decorating eggs with wax-resist dyeing, traditionally done before Easter.
A Japanese saw (pull saw) cuts on the pull stroke, producing thinner, more precise cuts than Western push saws.
Resin river tables, which embed a colorful resin 'river' through a slab of wood, became a viral woodworking trend.
A craft vinyl weeding tool removes excess vinyl from around cut designs before applying transfer tape.
The spinning jenny, invented by James Hargreaves in 1764, could spin eight threads simultaneously.
A moisture barrier like Thompson's Water Seal protects outdoor DIY wood projects from rot and weather damage.
Finger knitting is the simplest form of knitting, using only your fingers to create chains and simple fabric.
A bow drill was used by ancient civilizations to both start fires and drill holes, making it one of the earliest power tools.
Shrinky Dinks, sheets of polystyrene that shrink when heated, were invented in 1973 and remain a popular kids' craft.
A drawknife removes large amounts of wood quickly by pulling the blade toward you along the grain.
The craft of tin punching creates decorative patterns in thin metal sheets, popular in colonial American lanterns and pie safes.
A pottery kick wheel is powered entirely by the potter's foot, offering more control for experienced throwers.
Invisible thread, made of monofilament nylon, is used in quilting to stitch without visible thread lines.
A Kreg jig can create pocket hole joints in about 30 seconds, making it one of the fastest joinery methods.
The term 'DIY' (Do It Yourself) became popular in the 1950s when homeowners began doing their own repairs and improvements.
Handmade paper can be made at home by recycling old paper, blending it with water, and using a mold and deckle.
National Woodworking Month is recognized every January.
A leather swivel knife is held vertically and rotated to cut decorative lines and patterns in dampened leather.
The brioche knitting stitch creates a thick, reversible, ribbed fabric that looks the same on both sides.
Mosaic art dates back over 4,000 years to ancient Mesopotamia, where colored stones decorated walls and columns.
A silicone mold for resin casting can be reused dozens of times and releases cured resin easily without a mold release agent.
Winding a center-pull ball of yarn using a nostepinne (Norwegian winding stick) prevents tangling during knitting or crochet.
A planer thins boards to a uniform thickness and creates smooth, parallel surfaces for woodworking.
The Hepplewhite and Sheraton furniture styles of the late 18th century are still replicated by woodworkers today.
A pottery muffle kiln uses a separate inner chamber to protect ware from direct flame contact.
Tole painting is a folk art tradition of decorating tinware and wooden objects with floral and nature designs.
A tongue-and-groove joint interlocks boards edge to edge and is commonly used for flooring and paneling.
Embroidery hoops hold fabric taut while stitching and come in wood, plastic, and spring-tension varieties.
A candle mold can be made from silicone, metal, or plastic.
World Art Day is celebrated on April 15, coinciding with Leonardo da Vinci's birthday.
Leather tooling produces three-dimensional designs on leather by carving, stamping, and modeling dampened hide.
The fair isle knitting technique, named after a Scottish island, uses two colors per row to create geometric patterns.
A wood burning pen (pyrography pen) operates at 750-1050°F and comes with interchangeable tips for different effects.
Concrete crafting has become trendy, with crafters making planters, coasters, and candle holders from cement mixes.
A craft robo or cutting plotter is an earlier name for the desktop cutting machines now called Cricut and Silhouette.
Ribbon embroidery uses silk or synthetic ribbons instead of thread to create raised, dimensional floral designs.
The oldest known quilt, the Tristan Quilt, dates to around 1360 and is displayed in the Victoria and Albert Museum.
A carving mallet, typically made of round hardwood or urethane, drives chisels without damaging the handles.
Mica powder adds metallic shimmer to resin, soap, candles, polymer clay, and other craft projects.
The patchwork quilt tradition was born out of necessity, using fabric scraps to create warm bed coverings.
Jesmonite is a water-based acrylic resin used as an eco-friendly alternative to traditional polyester and epoxy resins.
A try square checks that woodworking joints are perfectly 90 degrees and marks straight cut lines.
The craft of pyrography can be done on wood, leather, gourds, and even paper with temperature-controlled tools.
Caning and rushing are traditional chair seat weaving techniques using natural materials like rattan or bulrush.
A thread tension dial on a sewing machine controls how tightly the upper thread is pulled, affecting stitch quality.
National Handmade Day is celebrated on April 1.
Sprang is an ancient textile technique similar to knitting but worked on a fixed frame, predating both knitting and crochet by thousands of years.
A wood finish like Danish oil penetrates the wood grain for a natural, low-sheen look rather than sitting on top.
Wax seal stamps have seen a renaissance in paper crafting and wedding stationery, using flexible wax that won't crack.
A pottery needle tool is used to cut, score, and incise designs in leather-hard and wet clay.
Live edge furniture preserves the natural edge of a tree slab, showcasing the organic shape of the wood.
Hand spinning on a spinning wheel converts raw fleece into yarn and has experienced a revival among fiber arts enthusiasts.
A tenon saw has a stiff brass or steel back that keeps the blade straight for precise joinery cuts in woodworking.
The Elmer's Glue company was founded in 1947, and their white school glue became a craft essential.
A pottery sponge on a stick reaches inside tall, narrow vessels to smooth the interior walls.
Reverse canvas is a technique where vinyl is applied to the back of a stretched canvas for a framed sign effect.
A craft fair or art market typically charges vendors between $50 and $500 for a booth, depending on the venue.
William Morris's Red House, completed in 1860, was a manifesto of the Arts and Crafts movement in architectural form.
Toggle clamps provide quick, repeatable clamping for woodworking jigs and fixtures.
Kumiko patterns like asanoha (hemp leaf) and shippō (seven treasures) have specific traditional meanings in Japanese culture.
A pottery extruder pushes clay through shaped dies to create consistent coils, tubes, and decorative handles.
Transfer paper allows crafters to copy printed designs onto wood, fabric, glass, and other surfaces.
A chamfer is a 45-degree bevel cut on the edge of a board, easily made with a router or hand plane.
Tapestry weaving creates images and patterns by selectively exposing colored weft threads across the warp.
The hot foil technique in card making presses metallic foil onto paper using a heated die for a shiny, embossed effect.
A leather edge paint seals and colors the raw edges of leather for a polished, professional look.
National Stamp Collecting Month is celebrated in October, and rubber stamp crafting is a related popular hobby.
A micrometer measures the thickness of materials like metal sheet, wire, and leather to thousandths of an inch.
Resin geode art uses pigments, crystals, and metallic flakes in resin to simulate the appearance of geode crystals.
The Japanese craft of kogin-zashi is a form of counted thread embroidery from northern Honshu.
A bench hook is a simple woodworking jig that hooks over the edge of a bench to hold boards for hand sawing.
Craft adhesives include white glue, super glue, epoxy, hot glue, spray adhesive, and double-sided tape, each with optimal uses.
The guilds of medieval Europe maintained strict apprenticeship systems for crafts like woodworking, weaving, and pottery.
A pottery bat pin secures throwing bats to the wheel head, keeping them centered during use.
DIY pallet furniture became a major trend in the 2010s, turning discarded shipping pallets into tables, beds, and shelves.
Stumpwork is a 17th-century English embroidery technique that creates padded, three-dimensional figures and flowers.
A Japanese plane (kanna) is pulled toward the user rather than pushed, producing gossamer-thin shavings.
The craft of enameling fuses powdered glass onto metal at temperatures of 1,400-1,500°F to create colorful designs.
Dimensional fabric paint puffs up when heated, creating raised designs on T-shirts and tote bags.
A pottery rib made from a gourd is a traditional tool for shaping and compressing clay on the wheel.
National Handwriting Day is celebrated on January 23, honoring John Hancock's birthday and the art of calligraphy.
A spline joint reinforces miter joints by inserting a thin strip of wood across the joint for added strength.
Indigo dyeing is one of the oldest textile coloring techniques, used for over 6,000 years across multiple continents.
A craft room pegboard organizes scissors, tape rolls, ribbons, and tools within easy reach on a wall.
The Amish quilting tradition is renowned for its bold geometric patterns, solid colors, and meticulous hand stitching.
A swivel chair caster cup protects hardwood floors, a common DIY accessory made from felt, rubber, or wood.
Needle-turn appliqué is a hand-sewing technique where fabric edges are turned under with the needle as you stitch.
A panel saw breaks down full sheets of plywood into manageable pieces, essential for cabinet and furniture projects.
The craft of whittling only requires a sharp knife and a piece of wood—basswood and pine are the most popular choices.
A pottery glaze test tile fires small samples of glaze combinations before committing to a full piece.
World Embroidery Day is celebrated on July 30.
A leather hole punch creates clean, round holes in belts and straps using rotating punch sizes on a plier tool.
Yarn swift and ball winder together transform hanks of yarn into neat, center-pull cakes ready for knitting.
A woodworking shoulder plane trims tenon cheeks and rabbets with precision for tight-fitting joints.
Fabric Mod Podge is specifically formulated to adhere fabric to wood, glass, and other hard surfaces permanently.
The art of quilling was practiced by French and Italian nuns in the 17th century to decorate religious book covers.
A craft embossing folder creates textured patterns on cardstock when run through a manual die-cutting machine.
Penny rugs, made from wool felt circles stitched onto a backing, are a traditional American folk craft from the 1800s.
A wood moisture content below 8% is ideal for indoor furniture projects to prevent future warping.
The craft of weaving on a rigid heddle loom is considered the best entry point for beginning weavers.
A pottery underglaze is applied before the glaze coat and remains visible through the transparent glaze layer after firing.
National Polymer Clay Day is celebrated on September 17.
Hand-forged blacksmithing projects like hooks, bottle openers, and knives have become popular weekend craft workshops.
A French cleat is a simple DIY wall-mounting system that uses two interlocking 45-degree beveled strips of wood.
Tissue paper flowers have been a traditional Mexican craft (flores de papel) for hundreds of years.
A Veritas low-angle block plane is considered one of the finest hand planes available for modern woodworkers.
Paper marbling (ebru in Turkish) floats pigments on thickened water to create swirled decorative papers.
A craft iron (mini iron) is used for small pressing tasks in quilting, sewing, and heat transfer vinyl projects.
The tradition of Ukrainian pysanky egg decorating uses a stylus called a kistka to apply melted beeswax.
A doweling jig aligns drill holes for precise dowel joints, one of the simplest forms of woodworking joinery.
Alcohol ink markers like Copic markers are the gold standard for illustration and coloring in paper crafts.
The craft of tintype photography has been revived by modern artists as a hands-on alternative to digital imaging.
String art uses nails hammered into a board with colored thread woven between them to create geometric patterns and images.
A pottery centering cone helps beginners learn to center clay on the wheel by providing a visual guide.
National Button Day is celebrated on November 16.
A leather edge slicker, made from wood or canvas, burnishes raw leather edges smooth using friction and gum tragacanth.
The Japanese art of gyotaku involves pressing fabric or paper onto an inked fish to create detailed prints.
